attributes.stem:
The Junior Doctor on the General Medical Ward is asked to review a 45-year-old woman due to a new onset rash. She was admitted three days ago with acute gout and has been commenced on allopurinol, colchicine, paracetamol, ibuprofen, and prednisone. She has no other significant medical history and took no regular medications prior to admission.

On examination, she appears uncomfortable. Her pulse rate is 110/min, blood pressure 125/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, SpO2 97% on room air, and temperature 38.2°C. There are painful erosions of her oral mucosa with crusting of the lips. Her trunk shows multiple tender, dusky red macules with central blistering. Several areas show separation of the epidermis. Her conjunctivae are injected.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following medications is most likely to have caused this reaction?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features consistent with Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS), characterised by mucosal involvement, epidermal detachment, and systemic symptoms. The timing of onset in relation to starting a new medication is typical.

Allopurinol is one of the medications most commonly associated with SJS/toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN). The risk is highest in the first eight weeks of therapy, particularly in patients with renal impairment or those started on high doses.

Colchicine, while commonly used for acute gout, rarely causes severe cutaneous adverse reactions. Its main side effects are gastrointestinal symptoms including nausea, vomiting, and diarrhoea.

Ibuprofen has been reported to cause SJS/TEN but this is much less common than with allopurinol. NSAIDs more commonly cause urticaria or fixed drug eruptions.

Prednisone is not typically associated with SJS/TEN. In fact, systemic corticosteroids may be used in the treatment of SJS/TEN, although their role remains controversial.

Paracetamol very rarely causes severe cutaneous adverse reactions. It is generally considered one of the safest analgesics with respect to cutaneous side effects.

Management of SJS includes immediate cessation of the suspected causative medication, supportive care, and often transfer to a burns unit. The mortality rate can be significant, particularly in cases that progress to TEN (>30% epidermal detachment).
learningPoints: Stevens-Johnson syndrome is characterised by mucosal erosions, skin blistering and epidermal detachment affecting <10% body surface area.
Common causative medications include allopurinol, carbamazepine, phenytoin, sulfonamide antibiotics, and lamotrigine.
Immediate cessation of the suspected causative medication is essential in Stevens-Johnson syndrome.
references: Halevy S, Ghislain PD, Mockenhaupt M, Fagot JP, Bouwes Bavinck JN, Sidoroff A, Naldi L, Dunant A, Viboud C, Roujeau JC; EuroSCAR Study Group. Allopurinol is the most common cause of Stevens-Johnson syndrome and toxic epidermal necrolysis in Europe and Israel. J Am Acad Dermatol. 2008 Jan;58(1):25-32. doi: 10.1016/j.jaad.2007.08.036. Epub 2007 Oct 24. PMID: 17919772.

Oakley AM, Krishnamurthy K. Stevens-Johnson Syndrome. [Updated 2023 Apr 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459323/
